reasoning: The SOFR 90-Day Average is a trailing indicator. The underlying Fed funds target rate was cut by 25-50 bps in September 2025, and daily SOFR is currently around 4.05%, significantly lower than the daily rates from 90 days prior (which were ~4.3%-4.5%). Because the 90-day average mechanically replaces older, higher daily rates with newer, lower daily rates, it is mathematically bound to continue decreasing steadily in the near term, even if the Fed holds rates steady. Furthermore, the Fed is overwhelmingly expected (99% implied probability) to cut rates again by 25 bps on October 29, 2025, which will push daily SOFR down to ~3.80%. This will steepen the decline of the 90-day average. To see an increase by November, December, or January, the Fed would need to unexpectedly hike rates massively to bring current daily SOFR back above the rates falling out of the 90-day window. Given the current easing cycle, the probability of this is extremely low. I have assigned the lowest allowable probabilities (0.05 to 0.08), with a slight increase over longer horizons to reflect the fundamental uncertainty of macro environments extending into early 2026.
